nameMeanings.name: Washbourne
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of Family Names in Britain and Ireland © University of the West of England 2016
meanings: English: locative name from Great and Little Washbourne (Gloucs) or Washbourne in Halwell (Devon). The variant Washburn could also be from Washburn (WR Yorks), but there is comparatively little evidence that this place yielded a surname.

variants.name: Washburn
meanings: English: habitational name from Great and Little Washbourne (Gloucestershire), or Washbourne in Halwell (Devon). The Gloucestershire placename derives from Old English wæsse (genitive wæssan) ‘wet place, swamp, marsh’ + burna ‘spring, stream’. The Devon placename derives from Old English wæsce ‘place for washing’ + burna.
History: John Washburn came to MA in 1626 and settled in Duxbury, MA, in 1632.

nameMeanings.name: of
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Chinese Young, Sung, Jung, Yong, Chang, Kwang, Sang, Dong, Hyun, Jae, Jin, Kyung, Jong, Chong, Chung, Byung, Jeong, Pyong, Myong, Hak, Jin Hwan, Sook, Yeong, Choon.

variants.name: O
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Chinese Young, Bo Young, Chae, Chong Hwan, Dong Kun, Ho, Hwan, Hyun, Jong, Jung, Ki Soon, Kwang.  Korean Min, Chong, Chol, Jung Ho, Myong, Sa, Sung Kyun.

Korean (오; Chinese character: 吳): historically, there were two Chinese characters for the O surname, but by the late Koryŏ period (14th century AD ), one of them had disappeared. Some records indicate that there are as many as 210 different clans for the remaining character, but only sixteen can be positively documented. All sixteen of these clans originate from the same ancestor, O Ch’ŏm (吳 瞻), who migrated to Korea from China during the reign of Shilla's King Chijŭng (500–514 AD ). O is a common surname throughout Korea. Compare Oh .

nameMeanings.name: Norman
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: Of Germanic origin, from nord ‘north’ + man ‘man’, i.e. ‘Norseman’. This name was in use in England before the Conquest, and was reinforced by its use among the Norman invaders themselves. The Normans were the inhabitants of Normandy in northern France, whose name is a reference to the Vikings who took control of the region in the 9th century. In the 11th and 12th centuries they achieved remarkable conquests, including not only Britain but also Sicily, southern Italy, and Antioch. In the Scottish Highlands it is used as the Anglicized equivalent of Tormod .

en.lifeSummary: When Norman Washbourne of Wichenford was born in 1205, in Wichenford, Worcestershire, England, United Kingdom, his father, John Washbourne IV Knight, was 25 and his mother, Lady Margaret Poher, was 20. He had at least 1 daughter with Elizabeth Kniton. He died in 1279, in Little Washbourne, Worcestershire, England, United Kingdom, at the age of 74.

ko.lifeSummary: Norman Washbourne of Wichenford(이)가 1205년 영국 잉글랜드 워스스터실르 위천퍼드에서 태어났을 때, 그의 아버지 John Washbourne IV Knight(은)는 25세였고, 그의 어머니 Lady Margaret Poher(은)는 20세였다. 그는 Elizabeth Kniton(와)과 적어도 한 명의 딸을 두었다. 그는 1279년 영국 잉글랜드 워스스터실르 리틀 와스h보우른에서 74세의 나이로 사망했다.

У него была по крайней мере 1 дочь от Elizabeth Kniton. Он умер в 1279 г. в местности литл вашбурн, ворсестиршир, Англия, Великобритания в возрасте 74 лет.
